After college, I worked for a high-frequency trading firm where the basic unit unit of time was the microsecond.
A single blink was long enough for our algorithms to open and close thousands of positions.
Thousands of positions in a snap of your fingers.
Each trade born and buried long before a human observer would have even noticed the screen had changed.
At the end of the day, we would grade our trading by looking forward into the future for every trade.
If we bought a stock at $5, we would have liked to see the market trading at $5.01 moments later.
10 seconds for a stock like NVIDIA was an eternity compared to a no-name stock.
Thousands of individual trades could have been entered and exited in NVIDIA and the time it took the no-name stock to update its price a single time.
There were levels to this eye-blinking metaphor.
We used event benchmarks instead of time benchmarks.
Every time someone executed a trade, canceled a bid, updated an offer, was counted an event.
Any change in the state of the order book reflected someone pricing in new information about the world.
We would then benchmark our trading to the market price, say a thousand events in the future.
For some stocks, this would be milliseconds, for others, minutes.
This normalized our performance benchmarks across all types of stocks and made us more effective in generalizing our strategies.
It was my first lesson that event-based perception mattered more than objective time.
So it isn't minutes, but rather changes in the order book events.
Concept carried with me as I left HFT and moved into crypto discretionary trading.
